full_text:
We sincerely thank the reviewers for their encouraging and invaluable
comments. We have now addressed all the comments and believe that the
manuscript has been significantly strengthened. Below are the
point-by-point responses (in blue).

The main improvements in the revised manuscript:

1. Improved resolution of tumor-specific expression of MHCII - with
 > **Xenium single-cell transcriptomics data, additional spatial
 > proteomics representations, scRNAseq and imaging data analysis**
 > excluding the possibility of artefacts/spill over and
 > demonstrating true MHCII expression in the tumor cells.

2. Additional compelling evidence supporting the role of tumor MHCII in
 > HGSC taking into account **tumor genomic features, anatomical site
 > variation and associated immune cell signatures.**

3. Additional validation on the effect of tumor MHCII independently of
 > MHCI.

4. New analysis demonstrating the **technical robustness of CEFIIRA**,
 > excluding the effect of overall feature variability explaining the
 > model performance.

5. Improved malignant-compartment specific immunopeptidomic analysis
 > showing **MHCII antigens linked to cytoskeleton and adhesion**
 > providing the mechanistic background of MHCII expression on tumor
 > cells.

6. **New data from functional ex vivo experiments confirming the
 > mechanistic role of MHCII in T-cell activation**. using a novel

kground of MHCII expression on tumor
 > cells.

6. **New data from functional ex vivo experiments confirming the
 > mechanistic role of MHCII in T-cell activation**. using a novel
 > platform of patient-derived immune-competent cultures (iPDCs) we
 > demonstrated that MHCII-high tumors exhibit enhanced CD8+ T-cell
 > cytotoxicity in response to PD-1 inhibition, and that MHCII
 > blockade abolishes T-cell activation and cytokine production.
 > These findings underscore the mechanistic dependency of T-cell
 > activation on MHCII, offering valuable insights for optimizing
 > immunotherapy strategies and improving patient outcomes in cancer
 > treatment.

This work presents several key novelties and impactful findings. Unlike
prior studies, which have only associated immune hotspot activation with
prognosis, we establish a direct link between intra-tumoral immune
hotspots and MHCII expression in cancer cells with single-cell spatial
resolution. We show, for the first time, that tumor MHCII expression is
spatially connected to localized immune activation and that immune cells
adjacent to MHCII+ cancer cells are tumor-reactive and more activated.
Additionally, our research reveals the spatially confined effects of
tumor-stroma interface composition, independent of tumor molecular
features. Crucially, we provide mechanistic evidence that tumors with

reveals the spatially confined effects of
tumor-stroma interface composition, independent of tumor molecular
features. Crucially, we provide mechanistic evidence that tumors with
high MHCII expression respond better to immune checkpoint blockade, with
this effect abrogated by MHCII blockade. These findings open new
therapeutic avenues, such as engineered immunopeptides or vaccines, and
highlight the importance of MHCII as a biomarker for more effective
patient stratification in HGSC. This work offers strong support for
future clinical validation in both retrospective and prospective trials.

**[Reviewer #1 (Comments to the Author ):]{.underline}**

Anniina Farkkila and colleagues report a single-cell spatial analysis of
metastatic high-grade serous ovarian cancer from 280 patients, including
929 single-cell maps. The authors identified heterogeneous
immune-environments at the tumor-stroma interface that are predictive of
clinical outcomes. Among these predictive factors, the authors newly
identified tumor cell MHCII expression as a major predictor of superior
outcomes and validated their findings with independent datasets.

In addition to a technological tour of force and the use of an extensive
cohort of patients, the main strength of the study lies in the
identification of tumor-intrinsic IFNG-dependent MHCII expression as

gical tour of force and the use of an extensive
cohort of patients, the main strength of the study lies in the
identification of tumor-intrinsic IFNG-dependent MHCII expression as
marker of T cell-mediated immune control of malignant progression in
human ovarian cancer. This is novel and biologically interesting. In
addition, the finding that lymphocyte (T and B cells) infiltration loses
predictive value after chemotherapy opens new avenues to investigate the
immunobiology of human ovarian cancer, the basis of which have been
established for treatment-naïve tumors before clinical practices
changed. Increased frequencies of immunologically cold tumors after
chemotherapy suggest a deleterious effect of chemotherapy on existing
anti-tumor immune responses.

MAJOR COMMENTS

-The small fraction of patients responding to ICIs in ovarian cancer has
been associated with the degree of genomic alterations, but not BRCAness
or HDR (PMID: 32923884, 40749672). Since the authors have DNA seq data,
how are genomic alterations associated with the immune signatures?

We thank the reviewer for this interesting question. To address this we
quantified the following genomic features: fraction of genome altered
(FCA), Tumor mutational burden (TMB), and number of HRD associated scars
(RRID:SCR_027138, [(1)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?T8YiQb))

features: fraction of genome altered
(FCA), Tumor mutational burden (TMB), and number of HRD associated scars
(RRID:SCR_027138, [(1)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?T8YiQb))
from our Whole Exome Sequencing and SNP-array data from our prospective
Oncosys-OVA trial samples. We associated these genomic features to the
immune infiltration patterns. Notably, we did not detect statistically
significant correlations in immune infiltration patterns related to the
above genomic alterations (**see new Ext. material - Fig.** **4**). In
our large multiomic cohort, the shallow whole-genome sequencing depth
was on average 0.5×, which provides a reliable estimate of HRD using
specialized bioinformatic tools
[(2)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?RyfLB6), however is not
sufficient to report mutations or CNVs or compute more detailed genomic
metrics, as previously reported
[(3)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?9QZhQZ). We have now added
these new results into the manuscript (**new** **Ext. material - Fig.**
**4**), manuscript lines **428 - 432**.

-The authors identified conglomerates of B cells and T cells compatible
with tertiary Lymphoid Structures. How is the presence of TLS impacted
by neoadjuvant chemotherapy?

The reviewer points out a very interesting point. In our previous
analysis shown in **Figure 2k**, we compared the abundances of recurrent

S impacted
by neoadjuvant chemotherapy?

The reviewer points out a very interesting point. In our previous
analysis shown in **Figure 2k**, we compared the abundances of recurrent
cellular neighbourhoods (RCNs) between PDS and IDS samples. The
abundance of RCN13, associated with B cells and T cells, and consistent
with tertiary lymphoid structures (TLS; see **Supplementary Fig. 2h and
Fig. 2e**), did not differ significantly between PDS and IDS samples.
However, it is important to note that the spatial profiling of TLSs in
our study is constrained by the size of the TMA cores analyzed, which
does not allow for reliable capturing of larger TME structures such as
TLSs, as also highlighted in a previous work
[(4)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?dTIM0C). We have now clarified
this limitation in the Discussion in "Study limitations" lines
**629-633**.

-Intrinsic expression of MHC-II in tumor cells opens several questions.
For instance, is there a predictive signature associated with CD4 T
cells specifically in tumors enriched in MHC-II+ tumor cells?
particularly in MHC-I-negative tumors?

We thank the reviewer for this insightful question. To address it, we
first examined our scRNA-seq dataset and compared CD4+ T cells from
MHCII-high versus MHCII-low tumors. This analysis did not, however,
reveal any differentially expressed genes between the groups (data not
shown).

and compared CD4+ T cells from
MHCII-high versus MHCII-low tumors. This analysis did not, however,
reveal any differentially expressed genes between the groups (data not
shown).

Next, we evaluated whether the functional states of T-cells are affected
by MHCII positivity of tumor cells with and without concurrent MHCI
expression. Interestingly, we observed significant differences in both
CD4+ and CD8+ T-cell states in the proximity of MHCII+ cancer cells
compared to those neighboring MHCII- cancer cells (upper panel, **new
Fig. 5f**). Both CD8+ and CD4+T cells showed significantly higher PD-1
expression in close proximity to MHCII+ tumor cells. Importantly, after
filtering out MHCI+ tumor cells, CD8+T-cells showed significantly higher
PD-1 expression (lower panel, **new Fig. 5f**) **indicating tumor
reactivity of CD8+T cells associated specifically with proximity to
MHCII+ tumor cells without MHCI+ expression.** These findings emphasize
the spatial relevance of tumor intrinsic MHCII expression and its
association with CD8+T-cell tumor reactivity specifically associated
with MHCII and irrespective of MHCI. These new results have now been
added to the manuscript (lines **490-494**).

-Also, increased frequencies of Th17 cells in MHC-II+ tumors, which show
better outcomes, demands more extensive analyses. How are Th17 responses
associated with outcomes in this cohort?

increased frequencies of Th17 cells in MHC-II+ tumors, which show
better outcomes, demands more extensive analyses. How are Th17 responses
associated with outcomes in this cohort? While many studies have
identified Th17 T cell infiltration with tumor-promoting activity, other
authors found a protective role specifically in ovarian cancer (PMID:
19470694). This could be a distinctive feature of this disease.

Thank you for raising this point. In our scRNA-seq dataset, we
identified 91 Th17 cells. Although we observed abundance differences as
noted previously in Fig. 4e, the small number of cells limits the
statistical power of survival analysis. To further investigate this
question, we utilized scRNAseq-based deconvolution and explored the TCGA
cohort (n = 253 tumors). Using scRNAseq, we constructed a Th17 specific
signature UCell score within the immune compartment consisting of 22
genes (*IL17A, IL17F, IL21, IL22, IL26, RORC, STAT3, BATF, IRF4, IL23R,
IL6R, IL1R1, IL6ST, CCR6, CCL20, RORA, AHR, IKZF3, CTSH, GZMB, IL2RA,
CD4*). Samples were then stratified into two equal-sized groups (low and
high) based on the Th17 signature. However, we did not observe a
significant association between Th17 score and patient survival. The
fact that tumor MHCII was predictive of survival in TCGA supports the
notion that tumor MHCII, and not the infiltration patterns of individual

Th17 score and patient survival. The
fact that tumor MHCII was predictive of survival in TCGA supports the
notion that tumor MHCII, and not the infiltration patterns of individual
immune populations, is the key feature predicting survival in HGSC.

![](media/image1.png){width="3.1496062992125986in"
height="2.5196850393700787in"}

-Also, trogocytosis often results in the incorporation of chunks of the
membrane of immune cells after long-term interactions (it could go in
both directions). It is unlikely that the frequencies of MHC-II+ tumor
cells identified by the authors are the result of this activity, but
expression of CIITA in tumor cells in association with MHC-II expression
at the protein level would further support intrinsic MHC-II
upregulation. Co-staining with a myeloid or B cell marker in the photos
presented in Figure 3 would also rule out co-localization of tumor cells
and MHC-II+ immune cells, if data are available.

We thank the reviewer for raising this question. To assess the
robustness of our MHCII+ cancer cell annotations, we first examined our
scRNA-seq dataset and evaluated the expression of immune markers
specifically within cancer cells (a **new** **Sup. Fig 4c)**. We did not
detect immune marker expression in any cancer cell cluster, including
those with high MHC-II expression. A small subset of cancer cells

lls (a **new** **Sup. Fig 4c)**. We did not
detect immune marker expression in any cancer cell cluster, including
those with high MHC-II expression. A small subset of cancer cells
expressing CIITA (60 cells) also lacked immune marker expression.
However, it is well established that transcription factor activity
quantification requires network level inference and typically does not
correlate with individual gene expressions
[(5)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?GuDIOl). Consistently, we did
not observe a correlation between CIITA and MHC-II expression at the
single-cell RNA-seq level. These results are now added also to the
manuscript in lines **391-393**.

To enable visual verification of tumor MHCII expression and its
distinction from immune cells, we have now also **updated** **Fig. 3f**
and included myeloid (CD11c) and B cell (CD20) markers in representative
MHC-II high tumor samples. In addition, we have now produced **Xenium
single-cell transcriptomics** from a high-MHCII tumor followed by
post-xenium tCycIF staining for tumor marker Pan-CK, and show that MHCII
transcripts are robustly expressed in the tumor cells (**new Fig. 3f**).
These are now added in the manuscript in lines **325-327**.

**[Reviewer #2 (Comments to the Author):]{.underline}**

In their manuscript, \'Single-cell spatial atlas of high-grade serous

e now added in the manuscript in lines **325-327**.

**[Reviewer #2 (Comments to the Author):]{.underline}**

In their manuscript, \'Single-cell spatial atlas of high-grade serous
ovarian cancer uncovers MHC class II as a key predictor of spatial tumor
ecosystems and clinical outcomes\' Perez-Villatoro et al. use a
multi-omics approach to identify cellular neighborhoods, cell types and
ultimately MHCII expression as prognostic in high grade serous ovarian
cancer. This is an overall well written manuscript which relies heavily
on omics analysis. Main strengths include a large cohort of patients
with important clinical variables and well annotated, use of multiple
omics approaches and some in vitro validation.

Major weaknesses include:

1\) Unclear what disease sites are being analyzed or how tissue site
(ovary, fallopian tube, omentum, etc) impact the down stream analysis.
This is a major concern given high levels of heterogeneity based on
tissue site.

We thank the reviewer for raising this important question. In response,
we have clarified the provided anatomical localization of all analyzed
samples in Supplementary Table 1. To address concerns regarding
potential confounding by diverse metastatic sites, we performed a
stratified sub-analysis focusing exclusively on samples collected from
primary tumor sites (ovary and fallopian tube). The performance of

diverse metastatic sites, we performed a
stratified sub-analysis focusing exclusively on samples collected from
primary tumor sites (ovary and fallopian tube). The performance of
CEFIIRA across 30 different splits of training and test sets from
primary sites only (**new S. Fig 3f**), for predicting long or short OS
in HRD samples, demonstrates:

i\) that the model's performance closely aligns with the mixed-site
dataset results in Supplementary Fig. 3f.

ii\) the top predictive features remain consistent, with MHCI, MHCII,
and E-cadherin emerging as the most informative.

**These findings indicate that our conclusions are robust and not
confounded by anatomical heterogeneity.** We have now added this
information into the Results section (lines **307 - 310**).

2\) While there is one in vitro study performed to validate the
differences in MHCII high vs low tumor cells, the vast majority of this
work is in silico without mechanistic investigation. There is no
postulated mechanism by which MHCII is upregulated in certain tumor
cells nor why stromal-tumor cell interactions may dampen this.

We thank the reviewer for raising this important point. To address this,
we have now conducted additional in vitro experiments (**new Fig.7a**)
demonstrating that blocking MHCII abrogates T-cell activation and
cytokine production using our unique platform of patient-derived

additional in vitro experiments (**new Fig.7a**)
demonstrating that blocking MHCII abrogates T-cell activation and
cytokine production using our unique platform of patient-derived
immune-competent cultures (iPDCs) (**new Fig 7d-f**). These findings
suggest a mechanistic role of MHCII in driving T-cell activation and
offer valuable insights for improving immunotherapy strategies to
optimize patient outcomes in HGSC.

Additionally, our new immunopeptidomic analysis demonstrates that MHCII
high tumors present antigens related to cytoskeletal and adhesion
proteins on their MHCII molecules. These proteins commonly generate
antigens likely via posttranslational modifications, providing a
mechanistic explanation to the MHCII expression in the tumor cells
**(new Figs 6e-i)**.

We have added these new data to the manuscript (lines **532-550,
553-571**) and the discussion (lines **611-613,** **619-620**). We also
display the mechanism supported by the data in our manuscript in a **new
Fig 7h**.

3\) The overall conclusions regarding hot-spots of immune activation
being prognostic in HGSOC is not novel nor are the findings that stromal
cells modulate the immune response.

We appreciate the reviewer's comment. We would like to clarify the main
novelties of our work. While prior studies have merely reported
associations between immune "hotspot" activation and prognosis in HGSC,

comment. We would like to clarify the main
novelties of our work. While prior studies have merely reported
associations between immune "hotspot" activation and prognosis in HGSC,
our work provides an important novel contribution by **linking
intra-tumoral immune hotspots to MHCII expression** **by cancer cells
with single-cell spatial resolution** (Fig. 4f, 4g, 4k). To our
knowledge, we are showing for the first time the spatially resolved
connection between cancer cell--intrinsic MHCII expression and localized
immune activation. We show that the tumor MHCII-adjacent immune cells
are tumor reactive and more activated. We also demonstrate spatially
confined effects of tumor-stroma interface composition **independent of
the tumor molecular features, which has not been shown before.**

Importantly, with our new experiments using our ex-vivo platform, **we
provide mechanistic evidence that these tumors are more responsive to
immune checkpoint blockade, and that this effect is abrogated upon MHCII
blockade.** This is an important observation, opening **new therapeutic
avenues for more effective patient stratification** for the existing
immune checkpoint therapies in HGSC, and **exploiting MHCII expression
using e.g. engineered immunopeptides.** Our work provides a solid
foundation for prospective patient stratification for immune checkpoint
blockade therapies.

ing MHCII expression
using e.g. engineered immunopeptides.** Our work provides a solid
foundation for prospective patient stratification for immune checkpoint
blockade therapies.

4\) In the multivariate analysis in figure 3K, the strongest positive
prognostic factor is primary debulking which does not align with the
strong clinical data of non-inferiority for neoadjuvant chemotherapy.
The authors should discuss this.

We thank the reviewer for raising this important point. We agree that in
several prospective, randomized trials
[(6)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?5oOgER), and also recently in
the TRUST trial [(7)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?LGcEg7), no
consistent OS differences have been shown for PDS and IDS surgical
approaches. However in the real-world setting, patients referred to
neoadjuvant treatment and IDS tend to have shorter OS due to the same
comorbidities or extent of disease that lead to the clinical treatment
decision [(8)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?yKmUOB). This has
also been observed in several large observational cohorts
[(8,9)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?yZnDyJ). As our multiomics
cohort is a retrospective, real world dataset, our findings are in line
with these findings. We have added a short discussion about this to the
manuscript (lines **631-633**).

cs
cohort is a retrospective, real world dataset, our findings are in line
with these findings. We have added a short discussion about this to the
manuscript (lines **631-633**).

5\) It is unclear what definition of homologous recombination deficiency
is being used.

We apologise for this unclarity. We utilized two validated metrics for
defining HRD based on CNV profiles detected using shallow whole genome
sequencing [(2,10)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?JsfAg3). A
description of this method for HRD can be found in the Methods section
"Molecular profile assignment" and **Sup. Fig. 1a**.

**[Reviewer #3 (Comments to the Author ):]{.underline}**

In this manuscript, the authors employ multiplexed imaging along with
other datasets to profile a large number of HGSC samples. They identify
various immune populations and define cellular neighborhoods. Several
lymphocyte populations are found to be linked to better OS and PFS.
Using their newly developed CEFIIRA tool, they discover HLA-II
expression on tumor cells as a key feature associated with longer
patient survival. HLA/MHCII expression is localized to specific
neighborhoods and associates with IFN signalling. In addition, it comes
with distinct MHCII peptidomes, differences in TCR clonality and
response to checkpoint inhibition in an co-culture screen.

associates with IFN signalling. In addition, it comes
with distinct MHCII peptidomes, differences in TCR clonality and
response to checkpoint inhibition in an co-culture screen.

The manuscript is strong in its scale and the use of a wide variety of
datasets, 253 patients are profiled in depth. The presented findings are
consistent with each other and align with previous observations from
other tumor entities. Overall, the presented analyses are of high
quality and presented in an appropriate manner. Most of the claims are
well supported by the presented data, with suggestions for further
improvements outlined below. Overall though I find this to be a strong
manuscript that will be of interest to the HGSC and spatial biology
communities.

Specific comments

Tumor cell expression of MHCII emerges as a top feature predicting OS,
however it remains elusive how much of this prognostic value is
independent of other associated variables. The authors should report an
adjusted model that adjusts for age, stage, therapy and molecular
profile. Another way of showing this would be to run their CEFIIRA
analysis leaving out feature classes (e.g. MHCII expression) and
reporting loss of predictive performance.

We thank the reviewer for this comment. The adjusted Cox model for OS,
adjusting for age, stage, therapy and molecular profiles is shown in

orting loss of predictive performance.

We thank the reviewer for this comment. The adjusted Cox model for OS,
adjusting for age, stage, therapy and molecular profiles is shown in
**Fig 3k,** showing that tumor MHCII remains predictive with HR 0.76 (CI
0.62-0.93). To further clarify our approach, we confirm that CEFIIRA
uses an established, cross-validated recursive feature elimination
(RFECV) to **identify the most informative predictors while adjusting
for the clinicomolecular factors**. After feature selection, we compute
permutation-based mean decrease in accuracy (MDA) scores, which quantify
the contribution of each variable by measuring the drop in model
performance when that variable is randomly permuted. This approach
leverages out-of-bag samples to provide an unbiased estimate of variable
importance. While CEFIIRA is not designed to produce traditional
regression-based adjusted effect estimates (e.g., hazard ratios), the
combination of RFECV and permutation importance allows us to assess the
relative contribution of each feature **while accounting for
correlations among variables.** We have now further described our
feature selection and interpretation steps in the manuscript\'s Methods
section (lines **934-935, 982-985**).

In my opinion, the paper would benefit from a more in-depth analysis of
tumor cell specificity of MHCII expression. Cancer cells have been

s
section (lines **934-935, 982-985**).

In my opinion, the paper would benefit from a more in-depth analysis of
tumor cell specificity of MHCII expression. Cancer cells have been
reported to express HLAII previously and the authors present a lot of
supporting evidence, however a lot of this data is also consistent with
the alternative explanation: bleed-over of MHCII signal from immune
cells into cancer cells. For example, if one was to assume such
spillover, one would expect the following: increase in signal in other
immune markers in cancer cells (this is actually the case), more MHCII
signal in highly infiltrated tumors (also the case) and close spatial
proximity of immune cells with MHCII higher cancer cells (also this is
reported here). I do not think that the presented deconvolution analysis
can clarify this issue as there are a lot of other potential sources of
inferred class-II expression from bulk data. scRNA data presented
supports the notion that cancer cells can express HLA-II but stops short
of showing that this explains the association with OS.

I would request a few additional QC metrics to rule out non-cancer cell
MHCII expression contributing here. For example, is there a correlation
between clearly immune related features (e.g. CD3 or myeloid markers)
and MHCII within cancer cells on the single cell level? There might also

For example, is there a correlation
between clearly immune related features (e.g. CD3 or myeloid markers)
and MHCII within cancer cells on the single cell level? There might also
be value in performing pixel-level analysis of MHCII signal with these
immune markers and potentially excluding immune-related pixels from
cancer cell masks.

We thank the reviewer for raising this important point. We are very
committed to ensuring that the cancer cell--specific signals we report
are not confounded by spillover from immune cells. To address this, we
have performed several new **analyses in our scRNAseq and spatial
proteomics data, and also produced Xenium single-cell transcriptomics
data.**

First, we quantified the degree of potential spillover in cancer cells
and assessed its correlation with MHC-II signal, as suggested. We did
not detect any correlation between CD3 or CD11b expression and MHC-II
levels in cancer cells, and we now include these results in a new
Supplementary Fig. 3g. Because high MHC-II expression could
theoretically arise from a combined spillover of CD3+ and CD11b+ immune
cells, we also tested the correlation between MHCII and the maximum
signal of these two markers. Again, no association was observed (see top
panel below).

In a complementary analysis, we examined the correlation between CD11b
signal in cancer cells and CD11b signal in neighboring immune cells

ion was observed (see top
panel below).

In a complementary analysis, we examined the correlation between CD11b
signal in cancer cells and CD11b signal in neighboring immune cells
(within 20 μm) and found a strong correlation (bottom left panel),
confirming the sensitivity of our approach to detect spillover when it
occurs. In contrast, this level of correlation was not observed between
MHC-II in neighboring immune cells and cancer cells (bottom right
panel), indicating that cancer cell MHC-II expression is not solely
explained by spillover. Moreover, in sc-RNAseq, we demonstrated that
there are no expressions of immune related genes in cancer cells
expressing MHCII (new Supplementary Fig. 4c).

Finally, we have now produced Xenium single-cell transcriptomics data
from a high-MHCII tumor followed by post-xenium tCycIF staining, and
show that CD74, a transcript of the MHCII complex is robustly expressed
in the tumor cells (**new Figure 3f**).

Although robust pixel-level spillover removal methods are not yet
available and are impacted due to the close proximity of immune and
cancer cells, **our multi-modal analyses collectively support that a
substantial component of the MHC-II signal detected in cancer cells
reflects a true cancer-intrinsic expression.** We have now also added
these new results to the manuscript lines **321-323**).

ponent of the MHC-II signal detected in cancer cells
reflects a true cancer-intrinsic expression.** We have now also added
these new results to the manuscript lines **321-323**).

![](media/image2.png){width="2.58125in" height="2.58125in"}

![](media/image6.png){width="2.5514905949256343in"
height="2.5458333333333334in"}![](media/image4.png){width="2.5541666666666667in"
height="2.5541666666666667in"}

The immunopeptidome analysis is much appreciated but if I understand
correctly, it was performed on bulk samples and not on sorted cancer
cells. Therefore, this analysis does not specifically inform us about
cancer cell antigen-presentation, which would be most relevant in this
context. If feasible, I would suggest to perform this analysis on sorted
cancer vs myeloid cells to precisely establish the cancer cell
immunopeptidome.

We thank the reviewer for this crucial point. Unfortunately, for MHCII
specific immunopeptidomics, a substantially large amount of cells (1-3g
of tissue corresponding to 1-3x10^9^ (billion) cells) would be needed.
Upon processing of the last 15 samples in our prospective cohort (from
1x2cm tumor piece) we have yielded on average 13 x 10^6^ (million)
cancer cells thus making this approach unfeasible.

To mitigate this limitation, we have now implemented a **new analytical
pipeline to enrich malignancy-associated MHCII peptides.** First, we

thus making this approach unfeasible.

To mitigate this limitation, we have now implemented a **new analytical
pipeline to enrich malignancy-associated MHCII peptides.** First, we
filtered the 60 genes mapped from the immunopeptidomic dataset by
excluding those present in the "HLA-II ligandome" of healthy ovarian
tissue [(11)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?tusUQL). We
acknowledge that this is not exactly the same as the fallopian tube, but
it does represent the same immuno-anatomical region to assess which
peptides are potentially presented on MHCII by tissue-resident APCs in
the female pelvic region.

This approach left us with 35 candidate genes with **no detectable
presentation in healthy tissue on peptide level**. We then used PRISM
deconvolution of bulk RNA-seq from matching 12 samples in iCAN study to
quantify expression specifically in tumor vs. immune--stromal
compartments (new **Fig. 6e**). This allowed us to evaluate (i)
tumor-fraction-specific expression (new **Fig.6g**), (ii) enrichment in
samples with high MHCII+ cancer-cell proportions (defined by matched
t-CycIF imaging) (new **Fig.6h**), and (iii) fold changes relative to
MHCII-mid and MHCII-low samples (new **Fig.6i**). **This analysis
revealed which malignancy-associated peptides are presented on MHCII
complexes.** Interestingly, many of these antigens were linked to

ow samples (new **Fig.6i**). **This analysis
revealed which malignancy-associated peptides are presented on MHCII
complexes.** Interestingly, many of these antigens were linked to
cytoskeleton and adhesion (*YAP, SNGC,* *TNS1, PKP2*), which are common
subjects to post translational modifications
[(12)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?qJCONK) with increased
immunopeptide potential
[(13)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?r3hfM0). This has been added
to the manuscript (lines **537-547**).

Prediction models performed better in HRP-like tumors than in HRD-like
counterparts. Is this explained by technical or biological factors?

We thank the reviewer for pointing out this observation. We evaluated
both technical and biological explanations for the observed better
performance of prediction models in HRP-like tumors. CEFIIRA was built
to minimise technical bias through i) strict sample-wise data
partitioning (preventing leakage), ii) stratified splits balanced by
therapy and molecular subtype, iii) nested cross-validation with
parameter tuning, iv) independent validation on unseen data, and v)
repeated (30×) consensus estimation. These controls make it unlikely
that technical artifacts explain the performance difference.

We also examined whether intrinsic biological heterogeneity might
contribute to the performance gap in OS predictions. To assess this, we

acts explain the performance difference.

We also examined whether intrinsic biological heterogeneity might
contribute to the performance gap in OS predictions. To assess this, we
quantified the variability of CEFIIRA input features within HRP-like and
HRD-like tumors by computing Shannon entropy and the Jensen--Shannon
(JS) divergence between the two groups (see graph of Shannon entropy and
Table with below). Using permutation testing (50 permutations), we did
not detect high differences in entropy or JS divergence for these
features, suggesting that **overall feature variability does not explain
the performance discrepancy supporting the robustness of our approach.**

These results strongly suggest that technical factors are unlikely to
account for the observed differences in predictive performance. The most
compelling explanation points to intrinsic biological differences
between HRP-like and HRD-like tumors. Remarkably, MHCII stood out as the
top feature, exhibiting the highest entropy difference between the HRD
and HRP groups, with significantly elevated values in HRD-like tumors.
**This underscores the substantial biological heterogeneity within
HRD-like tumors, further reinforcing the power of our integrative
approach in uncovering critical, clinically relevant features from our
multi-omics dataset.**

We have also added this result into the manuscript and Extended

r of our integrative
approach in uncovering critical, clinically relevant features from our
multi-omics dataset.**

We have also added this result into the manuscript and Extended
materials (lines **335-336**, Ext. Materials Table 1).

![](media/image3.png){width="6.267716535433071in"
height="4.083333333333333in"}

The authors should explain why they created separate CEFIIRA models for
tumor and immune cells? Can their approach handle multiple cell types at
once or is this information not retained when multiple cell types are
entered into the model? If the approach only works on one cell type at a
time, are there additional restrictions that have to be kept in mind
when performing multiple runs with potentially correlated features?

We thank the reviewer for this question. CEFIIRA can technically
accommodate correlated features and does so through RFECV-based feature
selection and permutation importance, both of which evaluate feature
contributions while accounting for multicollinearity. However, we chose
to build separate models for tumor and immune cells primarily for
interpretability. When multiple cell types are combined into a single
feature table, CEFIIRA identifies the most predictive features across
the entire dataset without distinguishing whether a given feature
originates from tumor or immune compartments. This makes it difficult to

fies the most predictive features across
the entire dataset without distinguishing whether a given feature
originates from tumor or immune compartments. This makes it difficult to
attribute the source of the predictive signal and to draw biologically
meaningful conclusions about cell-type--specific contributions. We have
now added this justification into the manuscript in lines **300-302**.

Lastly, the authors included a functional experiment involving iPDC
co-cultures to determine the effects of HLA-II expression on tumor cells
on immune cells. However, this functional link could be further
strengthened by blocking HLA-II to show that this has functional
consequences for immune cell behavior.

We thank the reviewer for this excellent suggestion. Using our ex-vivo
model [(14)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?lCHYa3), we now
utilized a blocking antibody to inhibit MHCII to investigate the
dependency of T cell activation on MHCII signalling. Prior to the
experiment, we confirmed that the iPDCs contained minimal contributions
of myeloid cells on the day of readout (day 5, Figure below shown for
three iPDCs).

Notably, in our new data, MHCII blockade completely abolished
PD-1-induced intracellular interferon (**new Fig. 7d**) and Granzyme B
(**new Fig. 7e**) expression in multiple T cell subsets. Additionally,

, MHCII blockade completely abolished
PD-1-induced intracellular interferon (**new Fig. 7d**) and Granzyme B
(**new Fig. 7e**) expression in multiple T cell subsets. Additionally,
multiplexed cytokine profiling of supernatant from the iPDCs confirmed
the loss of extracellular cytokine production after MHCII blockade
(**new Fig. 7f**). **These functional assays demonstrate the critical
role of MHCII in driving T-cell activation and local immune responses,
supporting the mechanistic dependency of T-cell activation on MHCII.**
This has been added also to manuscript lines **563-571**).
